comments:
Summary:
This paper presents an empirical study of several audio preprocessing strategies for deep convolutional neural networks applied to music tagging. The authors compare time-frequency representations (STFT vs. melspectrogram), logarithmic magnitude compression (decibel scaling vs. linear), frequency-axis weighting schemes (per-frequency standardisation, A-weighting, bypass), and simple multiplicative scaling. Experiments use a ConvNet (2D kernels) on the Million Song Dataset preview clips (~224k tracks), repeated runs to assess variance, and AUC as the main metric. The main conclusion is that logarithmic magnitude compression consistently improves performance, while most other commonly used preprocessing steps have little effect under their setup.

Strengths:
- The paper addresses a practical and under-explored question that matters for reproducible machine-listening experiments: how much do common preprocessing choices affect outcomes?
- Large-scale experiments using ~200k training tracks provide useful empirical evidence; code and split links are provided which aids reproducibility.
- The authors quantify run-to-run variance and set a reasonable threshold for what they treat as practically significant (AUC differences <0.005), which is helpful when interpreting small effects.
- The finding that decibel compression matters while other manipulations (frequency weighting, simple scaling) do not is a clear, actionable takeaway for practitioners.

Weaknesses / Limitations:
- Experiments use a single network architecture and set of hyperparameters. While this keeps comparisons controlled, it limits the generality of conclusions across model families (e.g., raw-waveform models, 1D convolutions, larger-capacity nets, recurrent modules).
- Only one dataset / data preprocessing pipeline is considered (MSD preview clips, downmixed/downsampled to 12 kHz, trimmed to 29s). The effects might differ with higher sample rates, different codecs, shorter excerpts, or other corpora (speech, environmental sounds).
- Reporting relies primarily on AUC averaged across tags; per-tag analyses or breakdowns by tag frequency/quality could reveal differential effects of preprocessing on different label types.
- Statistical treatment could be stronger: the repeated-run variance estimate is useful, but formal hypothesis tests or confidence intervals on pairwise representation comparisons (e.g., bootstrap over tracks) would increase rigor.
- Some experimental details are missing or under-emphasised (learning rate schedule, number of epochs, stopping criteria, whether models were tuned per-input type). These affect reproducibility and the interpretation of negative results.

Suggestions for improvement / future work:
- Evaluate additional architectures (including raw-waveform or larger models) to verify whether the conclusions hold across model families.
- Report per-tag or per-genre performance breakdowns, and/or analyse failure modes where preprocessing choices matter.
- Include more detailed statistical comparisons (e.g., bootstrap confidence intervals on AUC differences, significance tests across repeated seeds) and clearly report training hyperparameters and stopping rules.
- Explore other forms of compression or normalization (e.g., power-law, per-channel energy normalization variants) and different sampling rates or audio qualities.
- Clean up repetitive text and ensure the final manuscript is polished (the provided text contains some duplicated paragraphs and formatting artifacts).

Recommendation justification:
This is a solid empirical study with practical findings for the MIR community. It does not introduce major new algorithms but provides useful, evidence-backed guidance about preprocessing choices. Given the limitations (single architecture, single dataset) it is best framed as a practitioner-oriented contribution rather than a definitive theoretical result. I recommend acceptance as a poster (or short oral) so the community can build on and extend these empirical observations.
